data.title: National Grid Non-GAAP EPS of 73.30p, revenue of £18.38B; reaffirms long-term outlook
data.description:
• National Grid press release (NYSE:NGG): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of 73.30p.
  • Revenue of £18.38B (-7.4% Y/Y).
  • Financial outlook and guidance
     
    ■   Guidance is based on our continuing businesses, as defined by IFRS.
     
    ■   Financial outlook over the five-year period from 2024/25 to 2028/29:
     
    ■   total cumulative capital investment of around £60 billion;
     
    ■   asset growth CAGR1 of around 10% backed by strong balance sheet;
     
    ■   driving underlying EPS CAGR2 of 6-8% from a 2024/25 EPS baseline of 73.3p;
     
    ■   credit metrics consistent with current Group rating; and
     
    ■   regulatory gearing is currently 61%, trending back to the high 60% range by the end of RIIO-T3.
     
    ■   For 2025/26, we expect strong operational performance across the Group with underlying EPS expected to be in line with the 6-8% CAGR range from the 2024/25 baseline.

data.title: Cosan reports Q1 results
data.description:
• Cosan press release (NYSE:CSAN): Q1 net loss of R$1.8 billion, which is R$1.6 billion lower than the result presented in 1Q24.
  • Corporate net debt in 1Q25 totaled R$17.5 billion, R$6.0 billion lower than in 4Q24.
  • This reduction is mainly explained by the proceeds from the sale of Vale shares and liability management actions carried out during the period.

data.title: GlobalWafers to boost U.S. investment to $7.5B amid soaring chip demand
data.description:
GlobalWafers (NASDAQ:GFS), a TSMC (TSM) supplier, is looking to boost its investments in the U.S. to $7.5B amid increased demand as President Donald Trump pushes to expand domestic manufacturing.

The Taiwan-based semiconductor silicon wafer company plans to more than double its U.S. investment by $4B from a prior commitment to meet growing customer demand. The increased commitment was announced at the opening of GlobalWafers' new $3.5B wafer facility in Texas on Thursday.

Plans to build a state-of-the-art 300-millimeter silicon wafer factory in Sherman was first announced in 2022. At full build-out, the multi-staged, 3.2M sq. ft. site marks the largest facility of its kind in the U.S.

"Our U.S. customers appear to have a very strong demand for U.S.-based production capacity,” Doris Hsu, chairperson of GlobalWafers, told reporters.

During the opening ceremony in Sherman, the company announced plans to add two more phases to the site to expand production capacity. The campus currently has one operational phase and a second slated for construction, with room to support up to six phases in total.

Hsu noted that while there’s no fixed timeline for the additional phases, several conditions must be met before proceeding.

“Phases one and two must be profitable, and we need to secure customers... who show strong interest in local production and are willing to sign long-term contracts,” Hsu said.

The facility is part of the Biden administration-era CHIPS for America program, with the company set to receive $406M in federal grants for its projects in Texas and Missouri.

data.title: Finnair Oyj GAAP EPS of -€0.25, revenue of €694.2M
data.description:
• Finnair Oyj press release (OTCPK:FNNNF): Q1 GAAP EPS of -€0.25.
  • Revenue of €694.2M (+1.9% Y/Y).
  • Comparable operating result was -62.6 million euros (-11.6). Industrial action had a negative impact of around 22 million euros on the comparable operating result.
  • Net cash flow from operating activities was 192.1 million euros (138.9) and net cash flow from investing activities -57.2 million euros (-25.9).
  • Number of passengers increased by 2.6% to 2.6 million (2.6**).
  • Available seat kilometres increased by 2.3% to 9,126.4 million kilometres (8,922.9).
  • Including wet leases, ASKs increased by 1.6%.
  • Passenger load factor increased by 1.6 percentage points to 73.8% (72.1).
  • OUTLOOK AND GUIDANCE ON 29 APRIL 2025 Global air traffic is expected to continue growing in 2025. However, international conflicts, global political instability, the threat of trade wars and a tense labour market situation in Finland cause uncertainty in the operating environment. In particular, the risk levels related to tariffs between different countries and their direct and indirect impacts have increased.
  • Excluding the impact of industrial action, Finnair has planned to increase its total capacity, measured by ASKs, by c. 10% in 2025. The capacity estimate includes the agreed wet leases. Finnair has anticipated its revenue to be within the range of 3.3–3.4 billion euros and its comparable operating result to be within the range of 100–200 million euros in 2025.
  • In April 2025, industrial action is estimated to have a negative impact of around 15 million euros on revenue and around 10 million euros on the comparable operating result. In addition, regardless of the duration of industrial action, Finnair has decided to cancel 230 flights scheduled for the summer season due to a temporary lack of resources following the industrial action, which is estimated to negatively impact revenue by approximately 30 million euros and the comparable operating result by approximately 10 million euros. Based on the cancellations already confirmed, industrial action is estimated to have a negative impact of approximately 5% on the total capacity in 2025, measured by ASKs. Finnair will update its outlook and guidance in connection with the 2025 half-year report.

data.title: Swiss Re reports Q1 results
data.description:
• Swiss Re press release (OTCPK:SSREY): Q1 Revenue of $10.4B (-11.0% Y/Y).
  • Property & Casualty Reinsurance (P&C Re) net income of USD 527 million; combined ratio of 86.0%.
  • Successful P&C Re April 2025 renewals, with price increase of 1.5% achieved.
  • Corporate Solutions net income of USD 208 million; combined ratio of 88.4%.
  • Life & Health Reinsurance (L&H Re) net income of USD 439 million.
  • Return on investments of 4.4%; recurring income yield of 4.1%.
  • Swiss Re's capital position continues to be strong with an estimated Group SST ratio of 254% 4 as of 1 April 2025, above the target range of 200–250%.
  • Outlook: Swiss Re's Group Chief Executive Officer Andreas Berger said: "With a turbulent start to the year, we remain vigilant and focused on maintaining our strong foundations. Thanks to the decisive actions we took in 2024, all our businesses are well-positioned and have delivered a robust performance in the first quarter. Alongside our continued focus on cost discipline and efficiency, this gives us confidence in our 2025 targets despite a challenging environment."

data.title: Banco do Brasil S.A. issues guidance
data.description:
• Banco do Brasil S.A. press release (OTCPK:BDORY): Sees adjusted net income R$37.0 billion to R$41.0 billion in 2025.
  • Net Interest Income R$ 111.0 billion to R$115.0 billion.

data.title: BRF S.A. reports Q1 results
data.description:
• BRF S.A. press release (NYSE:BRFS): Q1 net income reached R$1.2 billion, double that of the same period last year, and net revenue reached R$15.5 billion, an increase of 16%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA grew 30% to R$2.8 billion, a record for a first quarter, while free cash flow reached R$1.3 billion.

data.title: Columbus Circle Capital Corp I prices $220M IPO at $10 per share
data.description:
Columbus Circle Capital Corp I (CCCMU) priced its upsized IPO 22M units at a price of $10.00 per unit. 

The company's units are expected to be listed on the Nasdaq Global Market under the symbol “CCCMU” and will begin trading on May 16, 2025.

Each unit consists of one Class A ordinary share of the company and one-half of one redeemable warrant, with each whole warrant entitling the holder thereof to purchase one Class A ordinary share at a price of $11.50 per share.

The Class A ordinary shares and warrants are expected to be listed on NASDAQ under the symbols “CCCM” and “CCCMW,” respectively.

Underwriters have a 45-day option to purchase up to an additional 3.3M units.

The closing of the offering is anticipated to take place on or about May 19, 2025.

data.title: Lazydays signals continued footprint optimization and cost reductions while reporting improved margins
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lazydays Holdings, Inc. (GORV) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Interim Chief Executive Officer Ron Fleming reported "encouraging progress" on Lazydays' two-part strategy: optimizing the dealership footprint and maximizing store performance. Fleming stated that the completed sale of five dealerships to Camping World in February and March enabled the company to deleverage its balance sheet by repaying approximately $145 million in debt. Fleming said, "The Camping World transaction also drove a $10 million year-over-year reduction in SG&A expenses due to reduced overhead personnel and marketing costs."
  • Fleming announced a letter of intent with General RV Center to divest three additional stores, aiming to add cash, reduce indebtedness, and further improve SG&A expenses. He noted, "Similar to our transaction with Camping World, we expect this transaction upon completion to add cash to our balance sheet, reduce our indebtedness, and drive continued improvement in overhead and SG&A expenses."
  • Fleming addressed macroeconomic concerns, saying, "While we are seeing some data suggesting decreased customer demand as a result of tariffs, we are optimistic that those concerns will diminish as we continue to see positive trends with respect to tariff relief."
  • Chief Operating Officer Amber Dillard highlighted sequential improvement in unit sales and gross profit margins. Dillard said, "Our new unit sales were up 18% in the first quarter versus the fourth quarter... our used unit sales were up 19% in the first quarter versus the fourth quarter." Gross profit per unit rose 39% quarter-over-quarter, and total gross profit margin reached 26%.
  • Chief Financial Officer Jeff Needles reported, "For the quarter, we achieved a gross margin of 24%, excluding LIFO adjustments, representing a 10% increase compared to the prior year. Gross profit for the quarter was $44 million, an increase of $6 million compared to the prior year period."

Outlook

  • Management reiterated its focus on further refining the dealership footprint, executing planned divestitures, and maintaining cost discipline. Fleming indicated continued evaluation of opportunities to "refine our footprint and reinforce our financial position."
  • Dillard stated, "We believe we can take actions to achieve improved performance without relying solely on market stabilization, as demonstrated by our first-quarter results."

Financial Results

  • Net sales for the quarter were $166 million, a decrease of $104 million or 39% compared to the prior year period, attributed to reduced inventory and store count. New unit sales were down 36% (912 units), while average selling price for new units was 15% higher. Pre-owned retail unit sales fell 48% (655 units).
  • New vehicle gross margins were 11% for the quarter, or 7% higher compared to the prior year. Used vehicle gross margins were 21%, or 10% higher year over year. SG&A expenses improved to $39 million for the quarter, compared to $49 million in the prior year period.
  • The company reported a loss from operations of $2.3 million, including non-cash impairment charges of $2.9 million. Excluding these, operating income would have been $600,000, compared to a loss from operations of $16.6 million in the prior period. Adjusted EBITDA loss was $4 million, an improvement from a $18 million loss in the prior year period.
  • Debt was reduced by $145 million during the quarter, including $95 million in floor plan debt, $47 million in mortgage debt, and $2.5 million on the revolving line of credit.

Q&A

  • NOT_APPLICABLE: No Q&A session was conducted or recorded following prepared remarks, as explicitly stated by management in both the operator's and CFO's opening statements.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts did not participate in a Q&A, so no analyst sentiment is available for this quarter.
  • Management's tone was confident and focused on operational improvement and financial discipline. Fleming described progress as "encouraging" and Dillard emphasized "tremendous opportunity to improve operational performance." The tone during prepared remarks was consistently optimistic, with an emphasis on execution and turnaround progress.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained a confident tone, but with more concrete results and cost reductions, suggesting growing optimism.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw management highlight tangible results from dealership divestitures, including significant debt reduction and lower SG&A expenses. In the prior quarter, the focus was on announcing strategic plans and recapping recent restructuring; this quarter emphasized execution and early success.
  • Gross profit margin improved from 19% in Q4 2024 to 26% in Q1 2025, while SG&A expenses fell from $53 million to $39 million. Operational focus shifted from managing through a challenging market to realizing benefits from footprint optimization.
  • Management's confidence increased, as they now cited specific improvements in margins and profitability drivers. Analysts' tone in the prior quarter was not recorded, and again, the current quarter had no Q&A.

Risks and Concerns

  • Fleming flagged potential demand headwinds related to tariffs, noting, "We are closely monitoring any potential price change for new RV units" and acknowledging "some data suggesting decreased customer demand as a result of tariffs."
  • Dillard highlighted ongoing monitoring of consumer demand due to "tariffs, supply chain shortages, and other macroeconomic trends remain uncertain."
  • Management reiterated a commitment to cost control, inventory management, and strategic divestitures to mitigate risks and reinforce financial stability.

Final Takeaway

Lazydays emphasized the progress made in optimizing its dealership footprint and reducing costs, resulting in improved gross margins and operating results for the first quarter of 2025. Management remains focused on executing planned divestitures, maintaining cost discipline, and monitoring external risks such as tariffs and supply chain challenges. The company signaled confidence in its turnaround strategy and its ability to deliver further improvements without relying solely on market recovery, supported by tangible results in margin expansion and debt reduction.
